divergesFromContract: Contract (feature-set/16-captains-tools.md) specifies that captain-only pages bounce non-captains to '/' via redirect rather than showing a locked view — they explicitly state 'There is no opacity-45/lock-icon treatment on this surface.' The CaptainLock component exists and is instantiated on S17 Captain mgmt, S19 Captain tools, and S17 Roster Iteration B (mobile), which implies an in-page soft-lock pattern (show the component in place of restricted content) rather than a hard server-side redirect. This is a design-vs-code divergence: the board treats captain gating as a visible inline lock overlay; the reference contract treats it as a redirect gate on at least the captain tools surface.

The body copy says 'Your rank doesn't have clearance for this view' — implying the locked surface is still rendered and partially visible to non-captains. The contract's redirect approach (rank !== captain → redirect('/')) means a non-captain would never see this component on those routes, making the component's use on S17/S19 either (a) for sections within a mixed-rank page where only a sub-panel is locked, or (b) an aspirational soft-lock pattern not yet implemented in code.

It instantiates no other reusable components. It is used in exactly three boards: S17 Captain mgmt (desktop), S19 Captain tools, and S17 Roster Iteration B (mobile) — always as an inline replacement for captain-only content panels rather than a full-screen takeover. The lock icon is lucide's 'lock' glyph coloured $primary; the circle background is a semi-transparent tint of $primary (hex #ff008c at ~18% opacity). No interactive elements; no button, no CTA. The component communicates a rank gate visually without providing any action path — the user can only navigate away.

notes: This board is a designer's field-kind reference sheet titled 'All 10 field-kind controls', one section per question kind in order. It is not a routable screen — it exists to validate and document the visual design of every input control. It instantiates exactly one reusable component (InputField, for short_text). All other controls are drawn as custom bespoke frame compositions, making this a useful source of truth for how each field kind should look. Two notable design divergences from the implemented code: (1) toggle is drawn as independent binary switches (multi-select semantics) but the code implements it as a segmented radiogroup (single-select); (2) scale is drawn as a segmented button row while the code uses a Radix Slider with mobile-vertical/desktop-horizontal dual layout. notes: This board is board #37 in the extraction index, explicitly labelled 'S17 Roster — Iteration B (terminal console)'. It is one of three roster takes on the canvas (board #26 S17 Captain mgmt, board #37 this board, board #38 S17 Roster Iteration B mobile). The 1040px width signals a desktop/tablet-wide table layout, making it a departure from the camp's mobile-first 430px constraint. The defining characteristic is the terminal console aesthetic: JetBrains Mono used for virtually all text, TermBar address widget, prompt '>' characters, and blinking cursor caret rectangles — a strong visual identity not present in any other board. The inline MemberProfile panel (below the table in the same scroll) is architecturally different from the reference contract's modal sheet; it also shows considerably richer member data (biography, arrival date, dietary, emergency contact, team colour pills, ID/passport in clear). Canonicality verdict: this board is an exploratory design iteration, not the canonical roster; it should be evaluated against the mobile-first Iteration B (board #38) and the original S17 Captain mgmt to select one canonical roster surface.

The board is non-redundant and unique — there is no other dietary/food-preference board in the canvas. Both the Dislikes and Allergies modules share the exact same 12 DIETARY_INGREDIENTS options and layout, differing only in their heading text and which items are shown as selected. The selected items (Onion/garlic + Chilli/heat in Dislikes; Peanuts + Shellfish in Allergies) are illustrative populated data to demonstrate the selected visual state. The checkbox-pill pattern (tinted background + filled check box) is a strong candidate for extraction as a reusable component since it appears across all OB Step boards that use multi-select question kinds. The board is 600px wide matching all other OB Step boards, not the 430px mobile-first width of the main app screens.
